context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, im stuck on the spider diagram for my city case study rn. idk what counts as enough info for the 5 pathways tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s narrow it down. What city did you choose?

A good test for “enough” is: for each of the 5 pathways, can you identify at least one city-relevant action, policy, project, or institution connected to climate action?

If not, which pathway feels emptiest right now? Name that one, and tell me the one piece of info you do have so we can see what’s missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than listing what to write. To move the student toward the assignment requirements, I’ll ask them to identify their city and infer what “enough info” means from the spider diagram’s purpose: mapping climate action across the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. I will avoid giving a filled-in template or sample content that could become part of their submission. Instead, I’ll define a practical threshold in question form—whether they can name a relevant action, actor, or evidence for each pathway—and ask them to test one pathway first.

exchanges.student: i picked Jakarta, but ngl all 5 feel kinda empty rn. i only have like flooding + sea level rise and idk if that goes in every section or what.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—flooding and sea level rise are a starting point, but those are mainly the problem, not all 5 pathways.

Ask yourself: what is Jakarta doing, and who is involved, because of those risks?

Try this first: list 3 concrete responses you’ve heard of in Jakarta—anything like infrastructure, planning rules, relocation, transit, energy, waste, community programs. What 3 come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should prevent the student from treating climate risks as if they fill all pathway categories, and guide them to distinguish the problem (vulnerability) from the city’s responses and actors. I’ll use Socratic questions that move directly toward organizing the spider diagram for Jakarta without supplying the actual content. I’ll keep it bite-sized and ask for a first sorting step.

exchanges.student: tbh i dont really know any, can u just explain all 5 pathways first so i can fill the whole thing in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you understand the framework, but you still need to do the city-specific mapping.

Think of the 5 pathways as broad buckets of urban transformation: what the city builds and uses, how it moves people, how land and nature are managed, how systems are governed/financed, and how people and communities are supported or included.

Now, for Jakarta, which seems most obviously connected to flooding: infrastructure, land/nature, governance, mobility, or community support? Pick one and we’ll unpack it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asked for an explanation of the framework, which is appropriate support. I should explain the 5 pathways at a high level without converting that into a completed solution for Jakarta. To remain Socratic and concise, I’ll give brief conceptual descriptions and then ask the student to sort likely Jakarta actions into them. This keeps ownership with the student and stays aligned with the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe infrastructure? idk tho, this still feels super broad and im more confused now tbh. that angle didnt help; can u give me like a simple example row instead?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re over-focusing on the labels. Bigger picture: each pathway entry just needs a few basics.

For one entry, think: 1) pathway bucket, 2) climate issue, 3) city response/action, 4) who is doing it, 5) where you found it.

So don’t try to fill the whole diagram yet. Find one Jakarta flooding response from a city plan or article, then tell me: what was done, and by whom?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated, so I should be a bit less Socratic while still not giving a direct solution or writing content for them. I will offer a generic structure for what one row/entry should contain—pathway, issue, city response, actor, evidence—without filling it with Jakarta-specific facts. Then I’ll ask the student to draft one rough entry from their own research. This reduces confusion but preserves the assignment work for the student.

exchanges.student: i havent found a source yet lol. can u just show me one made-up example row so i get the format?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—format only, not content for your assignment:

Pathway: [infrastructure]
Climate issue: [flood risk]
Action/strategy: [city expands drainage or coastal protection]
Main actor(s): [city government/public works agency]
Evidence/source: [name of plan, report, or article]

Now make your own Jakarta version: what source will you search first—city climate plan, resilience plan, or news about flood projects?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants formatting help, which I can support without writing their assignment. I should provide a clearly labeled generic placeholder example that is not city-specific and not usable as submission content, then immediately redirect them to generate their own Jakarta-based version. This keeps within the no-solution rule while reducing confusion.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe news? im kinda tempted to just do the table with super general stuff bc this assignment seems vague fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t hide behind vagueness. “General stuff” without evidence will make the diagram weak.

Use the table, yes—but each row still needs something specific and sourced.

Start with 2 source types only: 1 official city/government document, 1 credible news or NGO piece. Which one will you search first for Jakarta flood action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is considering a low-effort vague submission. I should set boundaries and steer them toward a minimally acceptable but evidence-based approach. I will not validate vagueness; instead I’ll emphasize that general claims without sources weaken the assignment. I’ll give a simple research strategy using accessible source types and ask the student to choose one. This remains concise and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: fine, official doc first i guess. but if i find one flood plan, can i basically reuse it across multiple pathways or nah?
exchanges.tutor:
Sometimes yes—but not by copy-pasting the same point 5 times.

One plan can touch multiple pathways if you focus on different parts of it: for example, physical works, land-use rules, governance, funding, or community impacts.

So ask: does the plan contain distinct elements, or are you just repeating one idea? What would count as two genuinely different elements?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking an important conceptual question about whether one policy can span multiple pathways. I should clarify that a single plan may touch multiple pathways, but the student should not duplicate it lazily; instead they should identify distinct aspects of the same plan relevant to different pathways. I’ll use a probing question to make them think about components rather than repetition.

exchanges.student: maybe like seawalls vs zoning rules? im still not sure what the tutor wants tho, like how different is “different enough” rn?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s better. Seawalls and zoning rules are different enough if they work through different mechanisms or involve different decision tools/actors.

Quick test: are they changing the city in the same way, or in clearly different ways? Are the main actors the same or different?

Answer that for your two examples in one sentence each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated a promising distinction, so I should affirm that and sharpen their understanding of what counts as distinct without turning it into a completed answer. I will define 'different enough' in terms of mechanism, actor, or pathway role, and then ask them to apply that standard to the examples they suggested. This keeps the student doing the reasoning.

exchanges.student: i guess seawalls change physical protection, and zoning changes where ppl can build. idk the actors tho, can u just tell me if thats enough and what else im missing?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that distinction is solid. Not a grade, but the idea has good potential.

What’s missing: 1) who makes/enforces each action, and 2) a source showing it.

When you open the official document, look for agency names and action verbs. For seawalls, which office would likely build infrastructure? For zoning, which office would likely regulate land use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for validation and guidance on missing pieces. I should confirm the conceptual distinction is strong enough without grading, then identify the next missing elements in the assignment structure: actors and evidence/source. I will avoid supplying Jakarta-specific actors or wording. To maintain Socratic guidance, I’ll ask targeted questions that move them toward finding those details in an official document.

exchanges.student: maybe public works for seawalls and planning dept for zoning? ngl can u just give me a super short checklist so i can stop overthinking this?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—likely those kinds of actors. Short checklist:

1) Pick the table option.
2) Find 1 official Jakarta source.
3) Pull 3–5 concrete actions from it.
4) Sort each action into a pathway.
5) For each one, note: issue, action, actor, source.
6) Reuse a source only if the pathway angle is genuinely different.
7) Then fill gaps with 1 credible outside source.

Now stop circling—what official source will you open first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overthinking and asks for a short checklist, which is appropriate support. I should provide a minimal process checklist tied directly to the spider diagram requirements, without giving content or wording for the assignment. I will keep it brief and practical, and end with a question that prompts action on the next concrete step.
